narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 56.8% chance that you would not pass—crossing the threshold we use to make admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and LSAT score of 35, alongside your Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles both at 2, your middle family income bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. Among students who passed the bar, the average Year 1 decile is 6.04 and Year 3 is 5.91, while the typical LSAT is 37.27 and undergrad GPA is 3.24—your numbers are solid in some areas but fell short in key predictors compared to those averages. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your Year 3 grade decile, which stood out as a major factor pushing the prediction toward expecting bar failure, likely because it signals challenges in sustaining strong performance later in your studies. Next in influence was your undergraduate GPA, which actually worked in your favor by nudging the prediction slightly toward passing, showing that your prior college record demonstrated real potential. Your Year 1 grade decile also contributed toward the failure prediction, reinforcing concerns about early academic momentum when viewed alongside the others. Overall, these top three elements—especially the later decile—outweighed positives like your full-time commitment, which aligns with 93% of bar passers, and your middle-income background, common among 35% of successful students. While your Hispanic background and identity as a woman weren't factors in the model's reasoning, they highlight the diverse paths many take to success in law. This isn't a judgment on your abilities or drive, but a data-driven flag that your profile statistically mirrors higher bar failure risks. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or above, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted prep or additional undergrad coursework, and gathering strong recommendation letters that speak to your resilience. Many applicants in similar positions reapply after addressing these gaps and get accepted—your story is far from over, and with focused improvements, you can shift the odds decisively in your favor.
